description: Closed taxonomy of propaganda/narrative techniques used to normalise free-text Gemini output (audit M-1). Each category defines a stable `id` (normalised code) and a list of `aliases` — synonymous phrasings observed in model output — used to map raw tags onto the controlled vocabulary so the coordination signal can match techniques across outlets reliably.
sources: DISARM Framework (disarm.foundation) — TA06 Develop Narratives / TA08 Conduct Pump Priming techniques for narrative-development and amplification categories
Classical propaganda technique taxonomy (Da San Martino et al., SemEval-2020 Task 11 'Detection of Propaganda Techniques in News Articles'; EU DisinfoLab) for rhetorical/framing categories

techniques.name: Loaded Language
techniques.description: Words/phrases with strong emotional implications (positive or negative) used to influence the audience beyond their literal meaning.

techniques.name: Name-Calling or Labeling
techniques.description: Attaching a derogatory or laudatory label to a person, group, or idea to make the audience accept or reject it without examining the evidence.

techniques.name: Repetition
techniques.description: Repeating the same message, slogan, or claim to reinforce it in the audience's mind regardless of its validity.

techniques.name: Exaggeration or Minimisation
techniques.description: Overstating or understating facts, consequences, or the significance of an event to shape perception.

techniques.name: Casting Doubt
techniques.description: Questioning the credibility, motives, or reliability of a person, institution, or source rather than engaging with the substance of their claims.

techniques.name: Appeal to Fear or Prejudice
techniques.description: Building support by instilling anxiety or panic about an alternative, or by exploiting existing prejudices and stereotypes.

techniques.name: Flag-Waving / Appeal to Patriotism
techniques.description: Justifying an action or position on the grounds that it serves national, ethnic, religious, or group identity and interest.

techniques.name: Causal Oversimplification
techniques.description: Assuming a single cause or reason for a complex issue when there are actually multiple contributing factors.

techniques.name: Slogans
techniques.description: Brief, striking phrases that may include labeling or stereotyping, used to provoke excitement and emotional reaction over reasoned analysis.

techniques.name: Appeal to Authority
techniques.description: Citing a source — official, expert, or otherwise — as proof for a claim without providing actual supporting evidence, or relying on weak/irrelevant authorities.

techniques.name: Black-and-White Fallacy / False Dichotomy
techniques.description: Presenting only two alternatives when in fact more options exist, framing the issue as a forced binary choice.

techniques.name: Thought-Terminating Cliché
techniques.description: A commonplace phrase used to dismiss dissent or end debate without addressing the substance of the argument.

techniques.name: Whataboutism
techniques.description: Discrediting a criticism by pointing to similar or unrelated wrongdoing by another party rather than addressing the original point.

techniques.name: Reductio ad Hitlerum / Guilt by Association
techniques.description: Discrediting an idea or actor by associating it with a widely despised person, group, or movement.

techniques.name: Red Herring
techniques.description: Introducing irrelevant material to divert attention from the main issue and away from the original argument.

techniques.name: Bandwagon / Appeal to Popularity
techniques.description: Persuading the audience to adopt a position because 'everyone else' is already doing so or believes it.

techniques.name: Obfuscation / Vagueness / Confusion
techniques.description: Using deliberately unclear, ambiguous, or convoluted language to obscure facts or avoid accountability.

techniques.name: Straw Man
techniques.description: Misrepresenting an opponent's position to make it easier to attack or refute.

techniques.name: Cherry-Picking / Selective Emphasis
techniques.description: Selecting only facts or data points that support a desired narrative while ignoring those that contradict it.

techniques.name: False Equivalence
techniques.description: Drawing a comparison between two things that are not actually comparable, implying equal weight or culpability where none exists.

techniques.name: Develop / Amplify Competing Narrative
techniques.description: Constructing or amplifying an alternative narrative that competes with and seeks to displace the established/factual account of events (DISARM TA06 Develop Narratives).

techniques.name: Conspiracy Theory Framing
techniques.description: Attributing events to secretive, malicious coordination by powerful actors without verifiable evidence.

techniques.name: Omission of Key Context
techniques.description: Leaving out information necessary to understand an event accurately, distorting the audience's perception through what is left unsaid.
